narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 63% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This prediction draws from key details in your profile, like your Year 1 school decile of 5, which is a bit below the typical student's 5.7; your Year 3 decile of 3, noticeably lower than the average 5.5; a middle family income bracket similar to many applicants; an LSAT score of 25, well under the usual 37; and an undergraduate GPA of 2.4, compared to the standard 3.2. You're also male, Asian, and planning full-time studies—demographics that align closely with the majority of students in our data, where most are white males attending full-time. The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your Year 3 decile, which nudged the prediction toward failure more than any other factor. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, also pulling in the same unfavorable direction. Your family income level came next in influence, followed closely by your LSAT score, your racial background, and then your undergraduate GPA—all contributing toward the higher failure risk in ranked order of impact, with gender and full-time status having the least sway. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your academic deciles and test scores stand out as weaker spots, placing you below average where stronger performers often succeed. While demographics like gender, race, and family income are harder to shift, you can realistically improve your odds by targeting changeable factors. For instance, excelling in any remaining coursework could boost future grade deciles, and retaking the LSAT to aim higher might help demonstrate stronger potential. Building a stronger undergraduate record through additional classes or certifications could also lift your GPA profile over time. Focusing on these steps, perhaps with targeted tutoring or study habits proven to predict bar success, positions you well for reapplying stronger next cycle. Keep pushing—you have control over the areas that matter most.
